key_reasoning: Two conjunctive components, both severely constrained by predetermined elements. Component 1 (multi-vessel Houthi commercial shipping attack causing ≥3 casualties): an active Houthi-US commercial shipping ceasefire has been in force since 6 May 2026 — a near-predetermined structural barrier. The deal persists because it serves Houthi operational interests; breaking it within 30 days requires a specific catalyst not currently visible. Even conditional on breakdown, a single operation causing ≥3 vessel casualties (sunk, severely damaged, or crew casualties) would represent a threshold Houthis have historically not crossed in a single coordinated strike. Component 2 (Article 5-equivalent Western navy declaration): 2+ years of extensive Houthi attacks on commercial shipping, including strikes near US naval escorts, have produced zero Article 5-equivalent declarations — this is a near-predetermined institutional impossibility, not merely an unlikely event. Current US strategic overextension in the Hormuz theatre, Saudi Arabia's refusal of base access (Day 69), and documented allied reluctance to escalate further entrench this predetermined element. Joint probability of both components: substantially below the prior.

predetermined_elements_bearing_on_scenar: Houthi-US commercial shipping ceasefire in force since 6 May 2026 — direct structural block on Component 1
Western navies have not applied Article 5 or equivalent to commercial shipping attacks in 2+ years of sustained Houthi operations — Component 2 approaches predetermined impossibility
US strategic attention is Hormuz-focused (Project Freedom, direct US-Iran naval clashes); Red Sea is secondary theatre
Regional alliance fracturing (Saudi base denial Day 69) limits Western military options and reduces incentive to escalate posture declarations
Iran's selective Hormuz transit system demonstrates a parallel maritime order in formation — Houthi ceasefire on commercial shipping is consistent with broader Iran-axis maritime strategy

scenario_quality_gate_findings.internal_consistency: Fails: the Article 5-equivalent clause is structurally inconsistent with 2+ years of established non-response precedent; the scenario implicitly assumes a threshold-breaking event that has not occurred despite many prior opportunities
scenario_quality_gate_findings.structural_difference_from_b4: Overlaps heavily with B4 (Regional Widening) but the Article 5 clause adds a Western formal commitment element not present in B4 and without historical precedent
scenario_quality_gate_findings.relevance: High — Red Sea shipping lane disruption has material economic consequences
scenario_quality_gate_findings.challenge_function: The scenario correctly tests whether the Houthi-US deal is durable and whether Western navies have a hard escalation ceiling

framework_specific.dissent_note: Prior of 0.03 likely weights Houthi attack capability without fully accounting for the active commercial shipping ceasefire as a structural brake. If the ceasefire is treated as a soft constraint rather than near-predetermined, the posterior rises toward 0.03. Wack methodology classifies it as near-predetermined given it is an operational agreement already in force with clear incentive structures on both sides.
